sentences.grammarNote: Resultative complements: verb + result. 起晚 = 'woke up late' (起 = get up, 晚 = late). Other examples: 写错 (wrote wrong), 听懂 (listened and understood), 说清楚 (explained clearly). The result directly follows the verb.
错过 (cuòguò) = 'to miss (an opportunity or vehicle)'. The 了 after the verb marks a completed (unfortunate) event.

sentences.romanized: Wǒ de lǎobǎn hěn bù gāoxìng.
sentences.grammarNote: 不高兴 (bù gāoxìng) = 'unhappy'. Chinese often forms negatives by adding 不 to a positive adjective: 高兴→不高兴, 开心→不开心. This is more natural than a single word like 'unhappy' in many cases.
感到 (gǎndào) = 'to feel / sense'. 有压力 = 'have pressure/stress'. Compare: 我很紧张 (I'm nervous) — using 有 + noun to describe a state is very common in Chinese.

sentences.base_language: I'm going for a short walk to clear my head.
sentences.romanized: Wǒ qù sàn sàn bù, qīngxǐng yīxià.
sentences.grammarNote: 散散步 is verb reduplication: 散步 (take a walk) → 散散步 (take a little walk). Reduplicating a verb suggests doing the action briefly and casually. It also softens the tone.

sentences.base_language: Tomorrow will be a better day.
sentences.grammarNote: 会 (huì) expresses prediction or expectation: 'will / is expected to'. 更 (gèng) = 'even more / more': 更好 (better, literally 'more good'). The sentence-final 的 in this pattern softens the statement into a confident assertion.
